items.correct: Colours opposite each other on the colour wheel that create strong contrast.
items.misconception: Colours that look similar and harmonise gently.
items.random_wrong: Colours used to flatter the artist.
items.why: Students confuse 'complementary' with 'matching nicely'. In colour theory, complementary colours (red/green, blue/orange, yellow/purple) intensify each other through CONTRAST, not similarity.

items.q: What is the first step of formal analysis?
items.correct: Description - noting what is actually present in the work.
items.why: Students jump to meaning immediately. Formal analysis follows a sequence: describe, analyse formal elements, interpret, evaluate. Description grounds interpretation in what's actually there.

items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.q: Whose triadic sign distinguishes icon, index and symbol?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peirce. “Saussure” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose 1964 Rhetoric of the Image analysed Panzani's pasta ad?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Barthes. “Metz” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Mitchell publish Picture Theory?
items.why: The real figure is 1994 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
